data.title: Boeing outlines $1B–$3B free cash flow outlook for 2026 amid higher commercial deliveries and Spirit AeroSystems integration
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Boeing Company (BA) Q4 2025

Management View

  • President and CEO Robert Ortberg stated, "we've set the foundation for our turnaround with stronger performance and record-breaking backlogs across our businesses," emphasizing ongoing progress but noting, "we haven't fully turned the corner." Ortberg highlighted the delivery of 600 airplanes and securing more than 1,100 commercial orders for the year, characterizing this as "one of our highest order totals ever."

  • Ortberg noted, "On 737, production is stabilizing at 42 airplanes per month and we're continuing to see improvement in the program as its on-time delivery performance has improved threefold compared with the previous year." He announced plans for higher production rates, including adding a new North Line in Everett and expanding the Charleston site for the 787, targeting a rate of 10 per month later this year.

  • Ortberg revealed a "transformational win to build the U.S. Air Force sixth generation fighter" and the completion of the Spirit AeroSystems acquisition, which is expected to "reinforce our efforts to improve safety and quality throughout our factories, operations and supply chain."

  • Executive VP of Finance & CFO Jesus Malave stated, "Revenue was $23.9 billion, the highest quarterly total reported since 2018. Revenue was up 57%, primarily driven by improved operational performance across the business, including higher commercial deliveries and defense volume." Malave added, "Free cash flow was positive $375 million, slightly higher than the expectations I shared last month, driven by higher commercial deliveries and working capital that improved compared to both the prior year and the prior quarter."

Outlook

  • Malave provided guidance for 2026: "we expect positive free cash flow of $1 billion to $3 billion, aligned with the expectations I shared last month. For clarity, this outlook contemplates an unfavorable impact of roughly $1 billion in 2026 associated with incorporating Spirit."

  • Management expects capital expenditures to reach "closer to $4 billion this year, including the incorporation of Spirit."

  • The outlook assumes "first quarter free cash flow will be a usage similar to first quarter of 2025, driven by normal seasonality," and that "the first half of 2026 to be a use of cash with the second half turning positive and accelerating sequentially."

  • Management stated, "we continue to believe the $10 billion free cash flow mark is very attainable, including impacts of the Spirit acquisition."

Financial Results

  • Malave reported, "BCA delivered 160 airplanes in the quarter and 600 for the year, the highest annual total since 2018. Revenue of $11.4 billion and operating margin of negative 5.6% both improved materially and primarily reflect better operational performance and higher deliveries compared to last year's results."

  • Backlog ended at a record-setting $567 billion, and BCA booked 1,173 net orders for the year.

  • BDS delivered 37 aircraft in the quarter, with revenue up 37% to $7.4 billion. The segment recorded an operating margin of negative 6.8%, impacted by a $565 million loss on the KC-46A tanker. BDS backlog reached a record $85 billion.

  • BGS revenue was $5.2 billion, with an adjusted operating margin of 18.6%. BGS orders totaled $10 billion in the quarter, contributing to a record $30 billion year-end backlog.

  • Cash and marketable securities stood at $29.4 billion, with a debt balance of $54.1 billion.

Q&A

  • Myles Walton, Wolfe Research: Asked about the duration and normalization of excess advances and customer considerations. Malave answered that "the excess advances over time actually will burn down quicker than what we expect on the consideration. So that will take a little bit longer to burn down on the 737 and 787 considerations."

  • John Godyn, Citi: Queried if normalized free cash flow could be higher than $10 billion. Malave replied, "I went through this in the fourth quarter. I'm very comfortable with our ability to achieve $10 billion... if you're asking me, can we be above 10%, I think the potential of our cash flow supports to be above 10%."

  • Douglas Harned, Bernstein: Asked about bottlenecks in production ramps and Spirit AeroSystems integration. Ortberg responded, "I actually don't think supply chain is going to be a big challenge for us in the next rate ramp from 42 to 47. But that's where we start to normalize with the supply base in terms of burning off the excess inventory."

  • Sheila Kahyaoglu, Jefferies: Inquired about BCA margins and Spirit's impact. Malave stated, "Right now, 737, 787 cash margins are depressed, and that's reflected in our free cash flow... we don't believe over time, that, that's going to materially impact what we believe and what we need to deliver on these types of cash flows."

  • Peter Arment, Baird: Requested 2026 delivery expectations. Malave guided, "Our expectation for deliveries on that program is around 500 aircraft" for the 737, and "anywhere between 90 to 100 aircraft" for the 787.

  • Seth Seifman, JPMorgan: Queried defense program charges and future outlook. Ortberg clarified, "the charge we took on the tanker in this quarter doesn't really reflect at all on any of the other BDS programs. It's a discrete charge against that particular program."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on cash flow normalization, production ramps, and program-specific risks, with a neutral to slightly pressing tone, frequently probing about the sustainability of improvements and timing of cash flow recovery.

  • Management maintained a confident and methodical tone in prepared remarks, while in Q&A, responses showed readiness to address specifics but occasionally hedged on timing and quantification, using phrases such as "we need to continue to see stability" and "as things potentially change, these could change as well."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts maintained a constructive but cautious optimism, with the current call showing more specificity in cash flow targets and integration plans.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language became more specific, with a defined 2026 free cash flow range and explicit mention of Spirit's integration costs.

  • Strategic focus shifted toward executing on production ramp-ups and integrating Spirit AeroSystems, compared to prior emphasis on certification delays and supply chain resilience.

  • Analysts remained focused on cash flow, margin recovery, and program execution, similar to the previous quarter, but pressed for greater detail on normalization timelines and quantifiable impacts.

  • Key metrics such as commercial deliveries, backlog, and segment revenues all showed improvement, with management expressing increased confidence in meeting long-term targets, while still acknowledging remaining challenges.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing risks related to delayed certification and first delivery on the 777X, customer considerations and excess advances on the 737 and 787, and the integration of Spirit AeroSystems.

  • Malave noted that "customer considerations for prior delays are not diminishing the pricing levels we are applying to new business" and highlighted that improvement depends on "production stability and continuous improvement in on-time delivery."

  • The KC-46A tanker program continues to pose risk, with a $565 million charge in the quarter, driven by higher production support and supply chain costs.

Final Takeaway

Boeing management emphasized that 2025 set a strong foundation, citing record commercial orders, delivery improvements, and backlog growth, while providing a clear 2026 free cash flow outlook of $1 billion to $3 billion despite anticipated headwinds from the Spirit AeroSystems acquisition. The company signaled ongoing investment in commercial production, a disciplined approach to program execution, and a commitment to achieving normalized free cash flow above $10 billion as operational improvements and integration efforts mature.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Eagle Financial Services signals consistent commercial loan growth into 2026 while emphasizing disciplined expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Eagle Financial Services, Inc. (EFSI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Brandon Lorey, President and CEO, reported that "our fourth quarter results reflect both the progress we've made throughout 2025 and the intentional way we executed our strategy." He attributed the linked quarter change in net income, which declined to $4.3 million from $5.6 million, primarily to lower net interest income and higher salaries and benefits. Lorey explained that these increases were anticipated as part of "our continued investment in our people."
  • Lorey highlighted stable credit quality, stating nonperforming assets ended the year at $14.6 million or 0.77% of total assets. He emphasized, "we remain confident in our collateral position and outlook."
  • The CEO noted that the company achieved its 2025 goals to "build a more granular and relationship-driven loan portfolio, grow core deposits and fee income... and expand in markets where we knew we could make a meaningful difference."
  • Organic loan growth was a key highlight, with $13.1 million in net loan growth for the quarter, mainly from commercial real estate and C&I lending. Lorey described the commercial engine as "resilient and scalable."
  • Kathleen Chappell, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "Last night, we reported net income of $4.3 million or $0.81 per diluted share for the fourth quarter of 2025." She added, "Our profitability remains solid with an annualized return on average assets of 0.91% and an annualized return on average equity of 9.18% for the quarter."
  • Chappell outlined an efficiency ratio of 70.3% in Q4 versus 64.1% in Q3, and net interest income of $16.4 million, down 4.8% from Q3. She attributed this to the "expected outflow of excess cash as the customer worked through the disposition of proceeds from the sale of their business."
  • Joseph Zmitrovich, President & Chief Banking Officer, described loan portfolio expansion by $13.1 million, driven by $67 million in total originations and $18.5 million of commercial loan growth, partially offset by a $10.3 million marine portfolio reduction. He stated the commercial loan pipeline is "up over $100 million when compared to January of 2025."

Outlook

  • Chappell expects wealth management fees and gain on sale revenue to "remain generally consistent with 2025 levels."
  • Chappell anticipates the efficiency ratio to "move slightly below 70% as Fed income continues to improve and salaries and benefits expenses move to a more normalized level."
  • Zmitrovich projected, "Looking ahead, we expect commercial loan production to remain consistent with our strategy."
  • Lorey stated, "We continue to engage in conversations with potential bank partners that align with our community-focused model and long-term strategic objectives," maintaining a disciplined approach to M&A.

Financial Results

  • Net income for Q4 was $4.3 million, with net interest income at $16.4 million. Noninterest income was $5.4 million, an increase from Q3's $5.2 million, driven by wealth management fees rising to $2.3 million, up 25% from Q3.
  • Noninterest expense was $15.5 million, representing an 8% increase over Q3, mainly due to higher salaries and benefits.
  • The net interest margin increased to 3.61% from 3.58% in the prior quarter.
  • Nonperforming assets slightly increased to $14.6 million or 0.77% of total assets from $14.3 million or 0.74% in Q3.

Q&A

  • NOT_APPLICABLE: The transcript does not contain a Q&A segment or analyst questions and answers.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' sentiment cannot be determined as there were no analyst questions present in the transcript.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone throughout, as evidenced by statements such as "I am genuinely proud of what our team accomplished this year" (President, CEO & Director Lorey) and "we feel well positioned to continue delivering balanced, high-quality results" (President & Chief Banking Officer Zmitrovich).
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remains upbeat, emphasizing resilience, scalability, and strategic discipline. There is continued confidence in growth, as demonstrated by repeated references to a strong foundation, momentum, and disciplined execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Net income declined from $5.6 million in Q3 to $4.3 million in Q4, with management attributing this primarily to lower net interest income and higher personnel expenses.
  • The efficiency ratio increased to 70.3% from 64.1%, reflecting higher operating costs and lower net interest income.
  • Noninterest income rose, mainly due to a 25% increase in wealth management fees.
  • Loan growth continued but at a slower pace, with Q4 net loan growth of $13.1 million compared to $21.3 million in Q3.
  • Nonperforming assets increased slightly, but management reiterated confidence in collateral positions and credit quality.
  • Strategic focus remains consistent with prior quarters, emphasizing organic growth, relationship-driven banking, and disciplined M&A.
  • Management’s confidence and tone are stable, continuing to highlight execution and opportunity.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "expected headwinds from marine runoff" impacting lending, with a $10.3 million reduction in the marine portfolio.
  • Nonperforming assets remain elevated compared to the prior year due to several large relationships moving to nonaccrual status, but management expressed confidence in the collateral position.
  • Higher operating expenses, particularly salaries and benefits, were anticipated and linked to investments in personnel and incentive compensation.
  • Lorey noted the company will only pursue M&A opportunities "that clearly enhance the strength and value of our franchise."

Final Takeaway

Eagle Financial Services closed out 2025 with stable credit quality, continued commercial loan growth, and a resilient, relationship-focused platform. Management remains confident in its ability to deliver balanced, high-quality results, underpinned by consistent execution, disciplined expense management, and a strong pipeline for further commercial lending. The outlook for 2026 emphasizes steady fee income, normalized expenses, and a focus on organic growth, with disciplined consideration of inorganic opportunities to enhance franchise value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Sysco targets at least 2.5% local case growth in H2 2026 as sales tools and strategic sourcing drive momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sysco Corporation (SYY) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO Kevin Hourican highlighted strong Q2 results, attributing performance to “improving case volume trends, strengthening gross margin performance and disciplined expense management.” Hourican announced, “we now expect full year adjusted EPS to be at the high end of our previously provided annual guidance range of $4.50 to $4.60.”
  • Sysco delivered nearly $21 billion in total revenue, with a 3% year-over-year growth rate, and positive case growth across all business units. USFS local case volume was up 1.2%, a 140 basis point improvement versus Q1. Hourican emphasized, “we expect reported local volume growth of at least positive 2.5% in both Q3 and Q4.”
  • The company completed the acquisition of Ginsberg's Foods, expanding its presence in the Northeast.
  • COO Greg Bertrand is transitioning to retirement, remaining as a strategic adviser for the next year.
  • CFO Kenny Cheung stated, “Our Q2 results were strong with sales growth of 3% and adjusted EPS growth of 6.5%. Our financial results this quarter also demonstrated high quality of earnings as free cash flow grew by 25% year-to-date.”

Outlook

  • Hourican projected, “we expect continued positive momentum for the second half of the year… we expect reported local volume growth of at least positive 2.5% in both Q3 and Q4.” He specified, “at least 2.1% to come from organic local case growth… with approximately 50 basis points additional contribution from M&A activity recently completed.”
  • Cheung updated, “we are confident in raising our FY '26 guidance to the high end of the adjusted EPS range.”
  • The company expects net sales growth of approximately 3% to 5% to $84 billion to $85 billion, driven by inflation of approximately 2%, volume growth, and M&A contributions.
  • Cheung stated, “we continue to expect our disciplined actions to generate strong gross profit dollars per case in margins in this backdrop.”
  • Shareholder returns are targeted at approximately $1 billion in dividends and $1 billion in share repurchases for FY '26, with repurchase activity resuming in Q3.

Financial Results

  • Sysco reported $3.8 billion in gross profit, up 3.9%; gross margin expanded by 15 basis points to 18.3%.
  • Free cash flow year-to-date was $413 million, up 25%.
  • Total U.S. Foodservice volume increased 0.8%, local volume increased 1.2%. International segment sales grew 7.3% (9.9% excluding Mexico divestiture), with local case growth of 4.5% and adjusted operating income growth of nearly 26%.
  • SYGMA segment saw 0.5% sales growth and 10.5% operating income growth.
  • Adjusted operating expenses were $3 billion (14.4% of sales), with a 15 basis points increase, partly due to investments and the impact of lapping prior-year incentive compensation.
  • Adjusted operating income reached $807 million; adjusted EBITDA was $1 billion, up 3.3%.

Q&A

  • Mark Carden, UBS: Asked about local case growth momentum and impact from winter storms. Kevin Hourican responded that Sysco’s performance “strengthened each month of Q2,” and January started strong, noting, “We have 3 new things, if you will, year-over-year… SC retention, sales consultant productivity… AI360 selling tool… Perks 2.0.” Cheung added that “numerous geographies already hitting our growth expectations.”
  • Jeffrey Bernstein, Barclays: Questioned assumptions behind the 2.5% growth guide and broader restaurant industry optimism. Hourican clarified, “we anticipate 2-year stack improvement in the second half versus the first half,” and expressed optimism regarding independent restaurant performance.
  • Lauren Silberman, Deutsche Bank: Asked about the mix of growth from new accounts versus penetration. Hourican said, “new customer win rate for Sysco in Q2 remains at all-time highs… improved colleague retention is driving improved customer retention.” Cheung noted highest growth of new and lowest level of loss in 12 months.
  • Jake Bartlett, Truist: Inquired about drivers behind increased EPS guidance. Cheung said, “the increase of EPS is actually not driven of D&A… all of the growth in the back half will come from self-help, things in our control.”
  • John Heinbockel, Guggenheim: Asked about drop size and SC capacity. Hourican responded, “penetration with existing customers is modestly down year-over-year but improved versus Q2.” Cheung noted retention improvement across all sales buckets.
  • Alexander Slagle, Jefferies: Asked about Sysco brand mix and value tier. Hourican described progress in Sysco brand penetration, with a focus on merchandising, pricing architecture, and sales force initiatives.
  • John Ivankoe, JPMorgan: Questioned AI and automation strategy. Hourican and Cheung described ongoing investments in automation and AI across business functions.
  • Danilo Gargiulo, Bernstein: Probed margin expansion. Cheung cited mix benefit from local case growth, strategic sourcing, specialty, and international as key drivers.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts adopted a positive to slightly optimistic tone, frequently highlighting Sysco’s local case growth, productivity initiatives, and the company’s ability to outperform the broader industry despite macro headwinds. Several analysts pursued clarity on the sustainability and drivers of growth, revealing constructive skepticism rather than negativity.
  • Management maintained a confident and assertive stance throughout both prepared remarks and Q&A. Phrases such as “we are confident,” “we expect,” and “we are very bullish” were prominent, with repeated references to self-help initiatives and structural improvements. Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was notably more assertive regarding outlook and the ability to deliver improved results amid industry softness.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was raised to the high end of the full-year EPS range versus last quarter’s reaffirmed guidance. Local case growth targets for H2 have increased to at least 2.5%, compared to a prior focus on sequential 100 basis point improvements.
  • Strategic initiatives such as the AI360 tool and revamped Perks 2.0 loyalty program continued to be emphasized, but management provided more detail on their impact this quarter.
  • The previous quarter highlighted stabilization and momentum building; the current quarter focused on acceleration, with clear metrics and new M&A (Ginsberg's) integration.
  • Analysts maintained a consistently positive tone, though this quarter's questions signaled increased focus on margin expansion and sustainability of growth.
  • Management’s confidence was more pronounced, repeatedly referencing internal drivers and specific growth levers versus a broader industry focus in Q1.

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO Hourican acknowledged softness in national restaurant segment volumes due to declining restaurant traffic.
  • Management flagged ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty and external headwinds, particularly weather impacts.
  • There was recognition of continued under-penetration in the value tier of Sysco’s product assortment, with ongoing efforts to close gaps through new merchandising strategies.
  • Customer loss ratio remains above historical best levels, with active initiatives to reduce churn and improve on-time deliveries.

Final Takeaway

Sysco’s leadership highlighted strong operational execution and momentum in Q2 2026, raising full-year adjusted EPS guidance to the high end of the range and projecting at least 2.5% local case growth in both Q3 and Q4. Strategic investments in sales productivity tools, expanded value-tier offerings, and the integration of Ginsberg’s Foods are expected to drive continued share gains and margin expansion, even as the company navigates persistent macroeconomic headwinds and evolving customer demands.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Pinterest's 15% job cuts could translate to $175M in annual savings: Evercore
data.description:
Shares of Pinterest (PINS) fell as much as 10.3% on Tuesday after the company disclosed in a Securities and Exchange filing that it would trim its workforce by less than 15% and shutter some offices.

The move is expected to impact roughly 700 or more employees at the social media company, which had a headcount of about 5,200 at the end of 2025. Pinterest is taking a ~$35M-$45M one-time charge related to the job cuts.

Analysts at Evercore ISI calculate the move is expected to generate up to $175M in annual cost savings, although they believe a material portion of the savings would be redeployed back into the business.

The research firm said the current share price weakness is indicative of concerns around the company's ability to reaccelerate growth in the U.S. and Canada this year. However, they also note that PINS stock currently has "very reasonable valuation support at these levels." 

Nearly 23M shares had changed hands by 2:00 p.m. ET on the NYSE, 1.5x its three-month average trading volume.

PINS has lost 27.8% in value in the past 12 months.

data.title: Applied Industrial Technologies projects $10.45–$10.75 EPS for fiscal 2026 while expanding M&A and strengthening margin outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Applied Industrial Technologies (AIT) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO Neil Schrimsher stated that "we continue to effectively manage through a mixed yet evolving end market backdrop during the second quarter," with sales and EBITDA margins aligning to guidance despite higher-than-expected LIFO expense and seasonally weak December sales. Schrimsher highlighted strong underlying margin performance, cost control, expanding backlogs, and business funnels, supporting a stronger sales trajectory into calendar 2026. The company reported organic growth of 2.2%, noting that November saw nearly mid-single-digit organic growth, but December sales rates were below normal seasonal patterns due to holiday timing. Schrimsher said, "December is always a noisy month given seasonal factors... This dynamic was further influenced this year by the midweek timing of the holidays." Early third quarter trends showed January organic sales up mid-single digits year-over-year. Orders in the Engineered Solutions segment rose over 10% year-over-year, the highest in over four years.

  • Schrimsher announced an 11% increase in the quarterly dividend and noted over $140 million deployed on share repurchases during the first half. He also announced the acquisition of Thompson Industrial Supply, with expected annual sales of $20 million, as a bolt-on to enhance the company's Southern California footprint.

  • CFO David Wells reported, "Consolidated sales increased 8.4% over the prior year quarter. Acquisitions contributed 6 points of growth... sales increased 2.2% on an organic basis." Wells added, "Gross margin of 30.4% was down 19 basis points compared to the prior year... During the quarter, we recognized LIFO expense of $6.9 million, which was $2 million to $3 million above our expectations and up meaningfully from prior year second quarter LIFO expense of $0.7 million." He highlighted that excluding LIFO, gross margins were up year-over-year and sequentially.

Outlook

  • Wells stated, "We are adjusting our full year fiscal 2026 EPS guidance following our first half performance and updated outlook. We now project EPS range of $10.45 to $10.75 based on sales growth of up 5.5% to up 7% and EBITDA margins of 12.2% to 12.4%." The updated guidance assumes LIFO expense of $24 million to $26 million and pricing contribution to sales of 210 to 230 basis points, both increased from prior guidance.

  • Organic sales for the full year are now assumed to increase 2.5% to 4% compared to the prior assumption of up 1% to 4%. Early third quarter organic sales trends are trending up by a mid-single-digit percent in January, but guidance remains prudent given ongoing macro and policy uncertainty.

Financial Results

  • Reported earnings per share was $2.51, up 4.6% from prior year EPS of $2.39. Free cash flow totaled $93.4 million, with conversion of 98% relative to net income. Cash on hand at quarter-end was approximately $406 million, and net leverage stood at 0.3x EBITDA. Share repurchases for the quarter totaled over 346,000 shares for $90 million.

  • Service Center segment organic sales increased 2.9% year-over-year, with U.S. operations up more than 4%. Engineered Solutions segment sales rose 19.1% over the prior year, with acquisitions contributing 18.6 points; organic segment sales increased 0.5%. Automation business organic sales increased 3%. Segment EBITDA margins faced headwinds from higher LIFO expense and M&A mix.

Q&A

  • Christopher Glynn, Oppenheimer, asked about Engineered Solutions orders and book-to-bill. Schrimsher confirmed organic orders were up over 10%, with book-to-bill above 1 for three of the last four quarters. Glynn also inquired about fluid power end demand trends, to which Schrimsher said, "the stock, destock, given how that's elongated out has really been worked through... we're also encouraged on the fluid power side of the amount of industrial activity."

  • David Manthey, Baird, questioned SD&A growth and share repurchase priorities. Wells explained that SD&A increase should come closer to sales growth next quarter due to merit increases and Hydradyne anniversary, and, "It's going to be the organic growth investment, followed by M&A, followed by the dividend increase... as well as then the opportunistic share repurchase."

  • Brett Linzey, Mizuho, asked about automation order drivers and pricing. Schrimsher described both pent-up demand and new projects as drivers, adding, "third quarter has the potential to be similar to the second quarter, so 250 basis points... in that."

  • Sabrina Abrams, BofA, probed the pricing guidance and LIFO assumptions. Schrimsher said, "We're seeing the announced increases from our suppliers as we think about for much of '26 are perhaps likely in place now... price moderates to that couple of hundred basis points in the fourth quarter compared to this 250 level that we have today."

  • Ken Newman, KeyBanc, questioned margin guidance and incremental EBITDA targets. Schrimsher replied, "potential for LIFO to be a 30 to 40 basis point headwind... Engineered Solutions, local account growth and Service Centers would both be the potential for benefit in that."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on clarifications about order momentum, pricing, margin dynamics, share repurchase, and capital allocation, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone.

  • Management maintained a confident tone about growth opportunities, margin execution, and capital deployment, but exercised caution around LIFO, inflation, and macro uncertainties. Phrases such as "we see that opportunity for that to play out and develop for us" and "we remain prudent with our guidance" were used, indicating measured confidence.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remains constructive but more focused on managing inflationary headwinds, while analysts showed ongoing interest in underlying growth and cost trends.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was updated to a $10.45 to $10.75 EPS range, higher organic sales growth expectation, and increased LIFO expense and pricing contribution assumptions compared to the previous quarter.

  • Strategic focus remains on organic growth, M&A, and capital returns, with new bolt-on acquisition activity announced this quarter.

  • Analysts continued to probe around incremental margins, LIFO impact, and sales growth, similar to the previous quarter, but management provided more specific breakdowns regarding pricing and segment performance.

  • Management tone was constructive but more detailed about inflation and cost controls than the prior quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified higher-than-expected LIFO expense, seasonally weak December sales, and ongoing macro and policy uncertainty as key risks. Mitigation strategies include active internal initiatives, ongoing cost control, and margin management.

  • Analysts raised questions around LIFO mechanics, SD&A trajectory, and the sustainability of pricing power and margin performance.

Final Takeaway

Applied Industrial Technologies management emphasized the company's execution through a mixed market environment, highlighting robust order momentum, strong margin discipline despite inflationary headwinds, and a focus on capital deployment across dividends, share repurchases, and acquisitions. Updated guidance projects higher EPS and organic sales growth, supported by continued pricing actions, new business funnels, and operational leverage, while acknowledging potential variability from macroeconomic and inflation dynamics in the second half of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Efsc outlines path to 6%–8% balance sheet growth and improved asset quality in 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Enterprise Financial Services Corp (EFSC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • James Lally, President and CEO, highlighted "For the quarter, we earned $1.45 per diluted share, which compares favorably to the $1.19 that we earned in the linked quarter and $1.28 in the fourth quarter of 2024." Lally emphasized the successful closure of a branch purchase in Arizona and Kansas, noting it "helped drive expansion of net interest income for the quarter to $168 million, which was a quarterly increase of $10 million when compared to the linked quarter and $22 million compared to the fourth quarter of 2024." He pointed out this acquisition "accelerated our strategy in 2 of our higher-growth markets by several years." Lally also reported balance sheet growth of 11% for the year and an increase in the quarterly dividend to $0.32 per share.
  • Lally signaled a strong focus on credit improvement, stating, "I can see a clear path for the elevated level of NPAs and OREO to reduce significantly in the next couple of quarters to more historical levels."
  • CFO Keene Turner stated, "We reported earnings per share of $1.45 in the fourth quarter on net income of $55 million. Excluding certain nonrecurring items, earnings per share on an adjusted basis was $1.36, a $0.16 increase from the third quarter adjusted earnings per share of $1.20."
  • Chief Banking Officer Douglas Bauche noted, "The completion of our branch acquisition and onboarding of new clients and associates has gone exceptionally well. We also successfully completed foreclosure of the previously highlighted Southern California real estate portfolio and are now one very important step closer to substantially reducing our nonperforming assets."

Outlook

  • Turner guided, "Moving into 2026, we expect net interest margin run rate to be roughly 4.2%." Lally outlined, "On the overall balance sheet growth, we're looking at 6% to 8%. And to your point, loans at about mid-single digit. But certainly, the deposit gathering rate and pace will exceed the loan growth pace."
  • Bauche indicated a target to reduce nonperforming assets from 95 basis points to a "more historically normalized level of 35 to 40 basis points over the next quarter or 2."

Financial Results

  • Net interest income reached $168 million in Q4, up $10 million from the prior quarter. Net interest margin improved to 4.26%.
  • Deposit growth for the quarter was $1 billion, with $400 million from organic sources and $600 million from the branch acquisition. Quarterly cost of deposits decreased to 1.64%.
  • Loans grew by $217 million in the quarter, driven largely by the branch acquisition. Tangible book value per share remained stable at $41.37. Quarterly net charge-offs were $20.7 million, compared to $4.1 million in the previous quarter.

Q&A

  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Asked about timing for reduction in NPAs and OREO. Bauche responded, "We received a favorable ruling from the bankruptcy court... in the middle of December, we were able to take 6 of the 7 properties into OREO... we're optimistic that by the end of the second quarter, we're going to see some resolution."
  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: Inquired about new nonaccrual loans. Bauche explained, "One is a retail center in Riverside, California... we're at a very good loan to value... The second loan... secured by a residential property in San Diego... we feel good about our position."
  • Damon Del Monte, KBW: Questioned expense and margin outlook. Turner confirmed, "We do see that $423 million stepping down to around $420 million. And then it just kind of hangs there." On provisioning, Turner stated, "I think our expectation is that charge-offs move down from the level they were at in 2025, and then that will alleviate some of the provisioning."
  • Brian Martin, Janney: Asked about fee income base and loan pipeline. Turner replied, "No, that's about right... So yes, I'm really just stripping out the $6 million and then growing off of that and adding the $0.04 or $2 million for the branches."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on asset quality resolution timing, fee income run rates, expense projections, and credit outlook, with a neutral to slightly positive tone as they pressed for specifics but acknowledged management’s detailed responses.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone, repeatedly emphasizing clear strategies and positive momentum. Lally stated, "I can see a clear path to improve credit statistics in the next quarter or 2." Turner expressed confidence in managing expenses and margin: "We feel pretty comfortable that... we're pretty well positioned to defend margin in that environment."
  • Compared to last quarter, analysts' tone remained cautiously optimistic but pressed harder on credit and expense clarity. Management was more assertive in projecting improvement in asset quality and operational efficiency.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from general confidence last quarter to explicit targets for net interest margin (4.2%), balance sheet growth (6%–8%), and NPA reduction.
  • Management’s tone increased in confidence, providing more forward-looking detail on credit resolution and operational leverage, particularly regarding the impact and integration of the branch acquisition.
  • Analysts’ questions reflected a sustained focus on nonperforming assets and operating expense run rates, with more probing on fee income sustainability this quarter.
  • Tangible book value growth and margin improvement continued, but credit costs and NPA resolution dominated discussions this quarter, whereas last quarter featured more discussion on integration and capital deployment.

Risks and Concerns

  • Elevated nonperforming assets, though management outlined a path to significant reduction within the next two quarters.
  • Expense growth related to branch acquisition and integration, offset by expectations for operational leverage.
  • Continued focus by management and analysts on potential credit migration and the timing of asset sales, particularly in Southern California.
  • Provisioning levels and net charge-offs, with management cautioning about possible volatility until full resolution of legacy credits.

Final Takeaway

Enterprise Financial Services Corp concluded 2025 with strong net interest income growth and successful execution of a strategic branch acquisition, supporting an 11% balance sheet expansion and tangible book value stability. Management outlined clear targets for 2026, including 6%–8% balance sheet growth, 4.2% net interest margin, and a return to historical asset quality levels, driven by anticipated resolution of key nonperforming assets. The company’s leadership expressed confidence in sustaining high-quality earnings and operational efficiency, with ongoing focus on organic growth, disciplined pricing, and proactive credit management as it advances into the new year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: First Merchants outlines 6–8% loan growth target for 2026 while advancing First Savings integration
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Merchants Corporation (FRME) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Mark Hardwick opened the call highlighting record total assets of $19 billion, total loans of $13.8 billion, and total deposits of $15.3 billion. Hardwick stated, "For the full year, we delivered record net income of $224.1 million and record diluted earnings per share of $3.88, an increase of 13.8% from the previous year." He emphasized the upcoming closing of the First Savings Group acquisition, which will add approximately $2.4 billion in assets and expand the company’s presence in Southern Indiana and the Louisville MSA. Hardwick confirmed, "We remain confident in our strategic and financial benefits of the merger, and we will actually close this weekend on February 1, 2026."
  • President Michael Stewart described 2025 as "a year of momentum and record results," noting robust commercial loan growth and strong deposit increases. Stewart reported, "$153 million in commercial loan growth for the quarter or 6% annualized, $852 million of increased commercial loan balances year-to-date, nearly 7% growth rate for all 2025."
  • CFO Michele Kawiecki noted, "Total revenues in Q4 were strong with meaningful growth in both net interest income of $5.4 million and noninterest income of $0.6 million. This resulted in overall pretax pre-provision earnings of $72.4 million, up $1.9 million from prior quarter." She also highlighted a 4% increase in tangible book value per share on a linked-quarter basis.
  • Chief Credit Officer John Martin remarked, "We had strong loan growth for both the year and the quarter of 7.3% and 5.8%, respectively, led by C&I loans shown on line 4, which grew nearly $700 million for the year."

Outlook

  • Stewart indicated that loan pipelines remain consistent, and he expects mid-single-digit loan growth for the first quarter of 2026. Hardwick added, "When you think about 6%, 7%, 8% is more how we think about the plan and consistent with what we delivered this year."
  • Kawiecki provided guidance that noninterest expense is budgeted to increase by 3% to 5% in 2026, primarily due to talent additions and the integration of First Savings. She stated, "We have 27.5% cost -- annualized cost savings that we've estimated that we think we can fully realize once we get past the integration."
  • On fee income, Kawiecki said, "We feel like we can get double-digit growth there. And so we're planning 10% growth."

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter net income totaled $56.6 million or $0.99 per share. The annual return on assets was 1.21% and annual return on tangible common equity was 14.08%.
  • Loan growth was reported at $197 million for the quarter and $939 million for the year.
  • The efficiency ratio was 54.5% for the year.
  • Deposit growth for the quarter was $424.9 million or 11.4% annualized, with a 12 basis point decline in the rate paid on deposits to 2.32%.
  • Net interest income for Q4 was $145.3 million, up $5.4 million from the prior quarter, aided by a $3.3 million recovery from a nonaccrual loan. Net interest margin increased 5 basis points to 3.29%.
  • Noninterest income totaled $33.1 million for the quarter, with customer-related fees of $30 million.
  • Noninterest expense for Q4 was $99.5 million, up $3 million from the prior quarter, including $500,000 of acquisition costs offset by a $700,000 reduction in FDIC special assessment accrual.
  • The tangible common equity ratio increased 20 basis points to 9.38% and 272,000 shares were repurchased for $10.4 million during the quarter.

Q&A

  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group: Asked about balance sheet optimization. Hardwick responded, "We're continuing to evaluate the possibility of some type of balance sheet kind of repositioning... What we have already settled on is that we do plan to sell the entire First Savings bond portfolio. It's about $250 million at close."
  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Inquired about loan growth expectations. Stewart replied, "Pipelines... remain consistent. So -- it's across the board when I think about geography or when I think about our segments, our C&I teams focused on different size companies are all in a good position."
  • Damon Del Monte, KBW: Asked about expenses and margin outlook. Kawiecki stated, "On a core basis, just looking at year-over-year noninterest expense, we have budgeted to increase about -- between 3% to 5%..."
  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: Sought clarity on fee income expectations. Kawiecki responded, "On the noninterest income basis for 2026, I mean, we feel like we can get double-digit growth there."
  • Brian Martin, Janney Montgomery Scott: Requested details about margin and expense integration. Kawiecki explained, "Core margin for the quarter, the interest recovery did add several basis points... seasonality definitely takes a dip in Q1."
  • Terence McEvoy, Stephens Inc.: Questioned multifamily construction credit quality and charge-offs. Martin said, "When I think about charge-offs, I think about it in that 15 to 20 basis point about depending on what we have in any individual quarter."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on growth, margin, and integration, with a generally positive and constructive tone, probing for clarity on expense management and strategic opportunities.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, frequently expressing optimism about growth, integration, and profitability, supported by direct statements such as "we remain confident in our strategic and financial benefits of the merger."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone remained upbeat, with increased focus on integration execution and cost realization.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter emphasized the imminent closing and integration of First Savings, with more granular guidance on loan growth (6–8%), noninterest expense increases (3–5%), and fee income (10% growth target).
  • Loan growth was lower than the prior quarter’s $268 million commercial growth, but pipelines remain steady.
  • Analysts' questions shifted toward integration, operating leverage, and the impact of new markets and talent acquisitions.
  • Management’s confidence appeared higher regarding the M&A integration, with more explicit guidance on synergies and expense control.

Risks and Concerns

  • Net charge-offs were $6 million for the quarter, with a $7.2 million provision. The reserve stood at $195.6 million, with a 1.42% coverage ratio.
  • The office loan portfolio remains limited at 1.9% of total loans, with $73 million maturing within a year.
  • Hardwick stated that any additional balance sheet repositioning would be "pretty modest," highlighting caution around liquidity and capital base.
  • Analysts raised potential risks around commercial loan credit quality, integration execution, and deposit repricing.

Final Takeaway

Management reiterated confidence in the upcoming First Savings integration, projecting sustained mid- to high-single-digit loan growth, double-digit fee income growth, and disciplined cost control for 2026. The company underscored its strong balance sheet, active share repurchase strategy, and focus on leveraging new market opportunities and talent to support ongoing shareholder value creation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Corning's deal with Meta should boost optical business: Morgan Stanley
data.description:
Corning's (GLW) $6B deal with Meta (META) to supply optical fiber to the latter's data centers should boost Corning's optical business significantly, Morgan Stanley said.

Corning shares surged more than 16% on the back of the deal, ahead of the company's fourth-quarter results, set to be released tomorrow.

“[We] were expecting there could be upward stock movement if there were to be news announcements ahead of earnings,” Morgan Stanley analyst Meta Marshall wrote in a note to clients. “While we thought an announcement could be more definitive on timing of scale-up investment, Corning's $6B agreement with Meta through 2030 for optical fiber, cable, and connectivity is of significant size for all potential markets (scale-up, - out, -across, but view as primarily scale-out).”

Marshall, who has an Equal-Weight rating and $98 price target on Corning, said that as concerns about power continue to rise, fiber getting closer to compute becomes paramount, with optical fiber solving the density and scale demands. Additionally, Marshall said that the company's optical business (which accounts for roughly 40% of overall revenue) is only poised to get bigger with this deal.

“Corning mentioned that Meta is currently not Corning's largest AI customer, but Meta will become the largest AI customer with this deal,” she wrote. “While we had not been expecting a raise to Project Springboard targets on Q4 earnings tomorrow, with the 30% enterprise Optical 23-27 CAGR staying in place given supply limitations, we now see more of an opportunity for the target to be raised to incorporate [the] META agreement.”

data.title: 'Google AI Plus' now available in the U.S. for $7.99
data.description:
Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) said Tuesday it is expanding the availability of "Google AI Plus," its mid-tier subscription plan for productivity and creativity tools, in 35 countries and territories, including in the United States.

The "AI Plus" plan starts at $7.99 monthly in the U.S., but first-time customers can avail the plan for $3.99 per month for the initial two months.

The plan will give users limited access to its AI models and tools like Gemini 3 Pro and Nano Banana Pro in the Gemini app, AI filmmaking tools in Flow, research and writing assistance in NotebookLM, among other things, when compared to its "AI Pro" plan, which is priced at $19.99 per month.

Existing Google One Premium 2 TB subscribers will automatically get full access to the benefits of the "AI Plus" plan in the coming days, the tech giant said.

Source: Blog

data.title: Apple plans to avoid iPhone price hikes due to memory dearth, but gross margins might take hit: TF
data.description:
The well-documented supply shortage of memory due to data center demand, which is leading to price spikes, will likely affect Apple's (AAPL) iPhone gross margins in the short term.

However, the Cupertino company plans to avoid increasing prices on its upcoming iPhone 18, according to TF International Securities analyst Ming-Chi Kuo.

"Higher memory costs will hit iPhone gross margins," Kuo said in a post on X Tuesday. "But Apple's playbook is clear: use the market chaos to their advantage—secure the chips, absorb the costs, and grab more market share. They'll make it back later on the services side."

Industry observers will closely monitor what Apple might reveal about the memory shortage in its first quarter fiscal 2026 financial report, which will be released post-market on Thursday.

"What Apple says could actually shake up other industries' stocks more than Apple's own or its suppliers'," Kuo said.

This is due to Apple's leverage in the supply chain and its ability to guarantee supply.

"iPhone memory pricing is now negotiated quarterly instead of every six months, so expect another hike in 2Q26," Kuo said. "Right now, the 2Q26 QoQ increase looks similar to 1Q26."

As of now, Apple plans to avoid increasing prices as much as possible when it releases the iPhone 18 series this fall, Kuo added. Still, the rampant AI server buildout might not only affect memory prices but other components as well.

Jefferies estimates that memory prices jumped roughly 50% last quarter, reinforcing supplier leverage across the AI supply chain.
